The assignment

Questions & Sample Answers

Question 1

[1] "What did Charlemagne, Holy Roman Emperor say about language?"
 [1] She said that having another language is like having another soul.                                                     
 [2] To have another language is to process a second soul.                                                                  
 [3]                                                                                                                        
 [4]                                                                                                                        
 [5] He said, “ To have another language is to possess a second soul.”                                                      
 [6] He said, "To have a second language is to have a second soul." He believed that language crafts and shapes our reality.
 [7] He said that to have another language is to have a second soul.                                                        
 [8] Holy Roman Emperor is said "To have a second language is to have a second soul."                                       
 [9] He said to have a second language is to have a second soul.                                                            
[10]                                                                                                                        
8 Levels:  ... To have another language is to process a second soul.

Question 2

[1] "What did Shakespeare think about roses?"
 [1] Shakespeare thought that a rose would smell as sweet even if it had a different name.                              
 [2] He thought that a rose by any other name would smell as sweet.                                                     
 [3] Shakespeare suggested that changing the name of something like rose does not change what it really is.             
 [4]                                                                                                                    
 [5] He thinks that a rose by any other name would smell as sweet.                                                      
 [6] Just as a rose by any other name would smell as sweet, reality remains unchanged regardless of what we call things.
 [7] Roses in any language smell sweet.                                                                                 
 [8] A2.He thought that a rose would smell as sweet even if it had a different name.                                    
 [9] A rose by any other name would smell as sweet.                                                                     
[10]                                                                                                                    
9 Levels:  ... Shakespeare thought that a rose would smell as sweet even if it had a different name.

Question 3

[1] "If you were German, how would you describe Rainbow Bridge, for example?"
 [1] I might describe it as strong, beautiful, and elegant.                                                                                                                                                                          
 [2] I will describe it as a beautiful thing.                                                                                                                                                                                        
 [3] I would describe it as beautiful or elegant.                                                                                                                                                                                    
 [4]                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 
 [5] I imagine that it is hopeful, fun, and bright.                                                                                                                                                                                  
 [6]                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 
 [7] With feminine words such as “Elegant” or “beautiful”.                                                                                                                                                                           
 [8] Because the word bridge is grammatically feminine in German, I would be linguistically predisposed to perceive it through more feminine words. For example, I might naturally describe the bridge as being beautiful or elegant.
 [9] In German, I would probably describe Rainbow Bridge with feminine words such as “beautiful” or “gracefull”.                                                                                                                     
[10] I would describe this as beautiful, elegant and fancy.                                                                                                                                                                          
9 Levels:  ...

Question 4:

[1] "If you were Spanish, how might you describe the Great Seto Bridge, for example?"
 [1] I would describe it as strong,powerful,or large.                                                                          
 [2]                                                                                                                           
 [3]                                                                                                                           
 [4] Strong and long.                                                                                                          
 [5] I would describe this bridge is strong, sturdy.                                                                           
 [6]                                                                                                                           
 [7] A4.I might describe the Great Seto Bridge as strong, long, and powerful because the word “bridge” is masculine in Spanish.
 [8] I would describe the Great Seto Bridge as strong and long.                                                                
 [9]                                                                                                                           
[10] - Strong                                                                                                                  
7 Levels:  ... Strong and long.

Question 5:

[1] "How is Japanese and English thinking different with respect to language? Give examples."
 [1] Japanese often depends on context. English uses more specific words. For example, Japanese may omit “he” or “she,” but English usually includes them.
 [2] In Japanese, we don't have to put the subject in front of the verb, but on the other hand, in English, we have to use the subject definitely.        
 [3] English is often forced on the person who did the action. However, Japanese is often focused on what happened.                                       
 [4] English speakers tend to identify the subject of a sentence.While Japanese speakers often omit the subject when it is understood from context.       
 [5]                                                                                                                                                      
 [6] English focuses on who did something. Japanese often focus on what happened. For example English - He broke the vase, Japanese -The vase broke.      
 [7]                                                                                                                                                      
 [8]                                                                                                                                                      
 [9]                                                                                                                                                      
[10] -                                                                                                                                                    
7 Levels:  ... Japanese often depends on context. English uses more specific words. For example, Japanese may omit “he” or “she,” but English usually includes them.


Summaries

five sample summaries

[1] Language can show deep ideas. Each language has its own sounds, words, and rules.Does the language we speak shape the way we think? To show this, she talked about the Aboriginal people. In their language, they do not have the words right and left. Instead, they always use directions like north, south, east, and west. This shows that language can change how we see things. Also, some languages do not have words for exact numbers, and the way people see colors is also different in each language. Many languages have male or female words as grammatical gender, and the choices are different depending on the language. In addition, the things people remember can change because of their language. She says we should think about how our language changes our minds.                                                                                                        
[2] Lera Boroditsky says the language we speak deeply shapes how we think. Human minds did not create just one way of thinking, but 7,000 distinct "cognitive universes" through 7,000 different languages. This diversity shows how flexible and creative the human mind can be. However, we are facing a big tragedy. We are losing about one language every week, and half of the world's languages could disappear in the next 100 years. Even worse, most scientific studies about the human brain are biased. They only test English-speaking American university students, leaving out almost all of humanity. This issue is not just about people far away; it is about you. The specific language you use every day guides your own memory, perception, and thoughts. Therefore, we must protect linguistic diversity to truly understand the human mind and see how we can think differently.
[3] She said people can share complex ideas through using languages. Thanks to languages, people can communicate with anyone, for example ancient people. However,there are over 7000 languages in the world and these languages use different pronunciation and words and structure. For this, one question is language's effects on type of thinking or not. She suggests one main example: Australians. They don’t use right and left, they only use east, west, north, and south. Because of this, they have an excellent sense of direction and always know where they are. The example also shows differences in thinking time. English speakers always think time left to right, however,Australians think east to west. For these examples, she concludes language's effects on not only communication tools but also shapes human thinking.                                                   
[4] There are over 7,000 languages in the world. Each has a different way of pronunciation and expression. Are humans influenced by the way they think by the language they speak? The Roman Emperor believed that the soul was different depending on the language he spoke, and Shakespeare thought that every language felt the same way. This discussion has been going on for a long time, but recent studies have shown data showing the answer. From the examples she showed, it can be seen that language is related to differences such as time, number, color, gender view, and logical thinking. Language affects the detailed sensibilities and value judgments of everyday life. There are as many ways of expressing and thinking as there are languages. Therefore, linguistic diversity should not be lost. You should think about why you feel this way about things.                 
[5] She explains how language influences the way people think.She argues that different languages encourage speakers to pay attention to different aspects of the world.Some aboriginal Australians languages use cardinal directions.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 
5 Levels: Language can show deep ideas. Each language has its own sounds, words, and rules.Does the language we speak shape the way we think? To show this, she talked about the Aboriginal people. In their language, they do not have the words right and left. Instead, they always use directions like north, south, east, and west. This shows that language can change how we see things. Also, some languages do not have words for exact numbers, and the way people see colors is also different in each language. Many languages have male or female words as grammatical gender, and the choices are different depending on the language. In addition, the things people remember can change because of their language. She says we should think about how our language changes our minds. ...

word count for summary


Opinions

five sample opinions

[1] I found this talk interesting because it shows that language is not just words but also affects thinking. I was surprised that even small differences in language can change memory and perception. It made me think more about how my own language influences how I see things in daily life.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 
[2] I found this TED Talk very interesting because it changed the way I think about language. Before watching it, I thought language was mainly a tool for communication. However, this talk showed that language can also influence how people understand the world. The example about accidents was especially interesting to me. English speakers often focus on who caused an accident, while speakers of some other languages focus more on the event itself. This suggests that language can affect memory and judgment. I had never considered that two people could witness the same event but remember different details because of their language. I also think the speaker raised an important issue about language diversity. If many languages disappear, we may lose unique ways of thinking and understanding the world. For this reason, protecting endangered languages is not only about preserving culture but also about preserving human knowledge and different perspectives.
[3] I think this video was very interesting. First, I was surprised by the Australian Aboriginal people. I have a bad sense of direction, so I think it is amazing that even a little child can always know the cardinal directions. Second, when I was a high school student, my English teacher told me that English speakers care a lot about who did an action. Watching this video, I realized it is true. Finally, when I study English, I usually remember the word as a simple translation, like “ringo” equals "apple." However, this video changed my view of learning foreign languages as learning a language is learning a new way of thinking.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       
[4]                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                
[5] I related the example about the accident. The speaker said English speakers remember who did it, like he broke the vase. However, Japanese speakers focus on the event and say the vase broke. I think this is very true. In Japan, if it is an accident, we do not want to blame a person directly. It is amazing that language changes what we pay attention to and how we judge blame. This video helped me realize that our daily language strongly shapes how we see the world.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           
5 Levels:  ...
